summary: Gruber recounts how his MacBook Pro's hard drive failed catastrophically, but he lost zero data thanks to a layered backup strategy using DiskWarrior, SuperDuper, and Dropbox. Disk Utility couldn't repair the drive, but DiskWarrior reconstructed a read-only volume from which he cloned everything. Dropbox provided an additional safety net by syncing his working files to remote servers, meaning even a worst-case scenario would have cost him very little. He uses the experience to deliver practical, urgent advice about backup strategies, arguing that every hard drive will eventually fail and users should prepare accordingly. He recommends SuperDuper's bootable clones over Time Machine alone, multiple external drives, and Dropbox for syncing and versioning. The post is essentially a public service announcement dressed as a personal war story.
key_points: A layered backup strategy (bootable clone + cloud sync + disk repair tool) meant zero data loss from a catastrophic hard drive failure
SuperDuper's bootable clones offer a significant advantage over Time Machine: immediate reboot from the backup instead of a lengthy restore process
Dropbox serves triple duty as syncing, remote storage, and file versioning, making it an essential part of any backup strategy
Multiple external backup drives are necessary — one isn't enough, because you may need to clone a recovered volume without overwriting your existing backup
DiskWarrior remains the gold standard for disk repair when Apple's Disk Utility fails, a recommendation unchanged since at least 2003
money_quotes: Every hard drive in the world will eventually fail. Assume that yours are all on the cusp of failure at all times.
It's a miracle to me that they work at all.
It is no fun at all to spend money on hard drives whose only purpose is to be used in case one of your regular drives fails. But when you need them, it feels like the best money you've ever spent.
I suspect the only people who aren't using Dropbox are those who haven't tried it.

Thanks to DiskWarrior, I lost nothing. Thanks to Dropbox and SuperDuper, though, the whole process was stress-free, because I didn't have much to lose even if DiskWarrior had not been able to salvage a non-corrupt image of the failed drive.
With SuperDuper, you just plug in the clone, reboot, and you're back up.
It's good to be spooked about how long your hard drives will last.
key_insight: A robust backup strategy requires multiple redundant layers — bootable clones, cloud syncing, and disk repair tools — because no single solution covers every failure scenario.
